chapter_summary: A sneaky serpent tricks Eve into eating fruit from the one tree God said not to touch. Adam eats it too. They feel ashamed and hide from God. God asks what happened, and they both make excuses. God tells the serpent, Adam, and Eve what will change. But in the middle of the sad news, God makes a wonderful promise: one day, someone from Eve's family will defeat the serpent forever — and that person is Jesus! God also makes clothes for Adam and Eve, showing He still loves them even when they disobey.
main_lesson: Even when we disobey God, He still loves us and has a plan to rescue us.

memory_verse.kjv: And I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed; it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel.
memory_verse.little_bible: God made a promise that one day someone from Eve's family would come and defeat the serpent — and that person is Jesus!

parent_guide: This is a foundational chapter for understanding sin and salvation. The 'seed' in verse 15 is the first gospel promise in the Bible, pointing forward to Christ — theologians call it the proto-evangelium ('first gospel'). God's making of garments in verse 21 shows grace: even in judgment, He covers their shame. Approach with gentleness; children can understand 'we all sometimes disobey God' without heavy theological guilt. Key themes to highlight: (1) Sin has consequences, but God is not done with us. (2) God always keeps His promises. (3) Jesus is the promised rescuer from the very beginning of the Bible.
application_for_children: When you do something wrong, don't hide from God — tell Him. He already knows, He still loves you, and He has made a way to forgive you through Jesus.

verses.kjv: Now the serpent was more subtil than any beast of the field which the LORD God had made. And he said unto the woman, Yea, hath God said, Ye shall not eat of every tree of the garden?
verses.little_bible: A sneaky serpent came to Eve. He asked a tricky question: 'Did God really say you can't eat from any tree?'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: A sneaky serpent asks Eve a tricky question about God.
verses.meaning: The serpent's strategy was not force but doubt — he twisted God's words to make Eve question what God actually said. Deception often starts with small distortions of truth.
verses.memory_phrase: The serpent asked a tricky question.
verses.prayer: God, help me know Your words so I am not tricked by lies.
verses.discussion_question: What do you do when someone tries to trick you?
verses.family_discussion: How can knowing what God really says protect us from being tricked by lies?
verses.do_it_today: Learn one thing God says that is true — like 'God loves me' — and say it out loud today.
verses.illustration_prompt: A bright, lush garden with Eve standing near a large tree; a colourful serpent is coiled on a branch, speaking with a sly expression. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And the woman said unto the serpent, We may eat of the fruit of the trees of the garden:
verses.little_bible: Eve told the serpent, 'We can eat fruit from the trees in the garden.'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Eve says they can eat from the trees.
verses.meaning: Eve begins by correctly recalling part of what God said. Knowing the truth is the first defence against deception.
verses.memory_phrase: We may eat from the trees.
verses.prayer: God, help me remember what You have said.
verses.discussion_question: Eve started by saying what was true. Can you remember a rule someone gave you that kept you safe?
verses.family_discussion: Why is it important to know what God actually says, not just almost what He says?
verses.do_it_today: Tell someone in your family one thing you know God says is true.
verses.illustration_prompt: Eve standing confidently near fruit-filled trees, gesturing toward them as she speaks; the serpent listens attentively on a nearby branch. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: But of the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the garden, God hath said, Ye shall not eat of it, neither shall ye touch it, lest ye die.
verses.little_bible: Eve said, 'But God told us not to eat fruit from the tree in the middle of the garden — or even touch it — or we will die.'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God said do not eat from that tree or you will die.
verses.meaning: Eve knew the command but may have added 'neither shall ye touch it' — showing how rules can become blurry when we repeat them. Knowing God's Word precisely matters.
verses.memory_phrase: God said do not eat from that tree.
verses.prayer: God, help me listen carefully to what You say.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever misremembered a rule? What happened?
verses.family_discussion: Why does getting God's words exactly right matter? How can we help our children know the Bible accurately?
verses.do_it_today: Ask a parent to read you a Bible verse and try to remember it exactly.
verses.illustration_prompt: A beautiful tree at the centre of the garden with golden fruit glowing gently; Eve points to it with a cautious expression. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And the serpent said unto the woman, Ye shall not surely die:
verses.little_bible: The serpent said, 'You will NOT die!' He was telling a lie.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: The serpent says, 'You will not die!' That is a lie.
verses.meaning: The serpent directly contradicted God's word. A lie that sounds confident can feel like truth — but the source matters more than the tone.
verses.memory_phrase: The serpent told a lie.
verses.prayer: God, help me trust Your words more than tricky lies.
verses.discussion_question: How can you tell if someone is telling you a lie?
verses.family_discussion: Satan's lie sounded very sure. How do we teach children to test what they hear against what God says?
verses.do_it_today: If someone tells you something that doesn't sound right today, ask a parent or trusted adult to help you check.
verses.illustration_prompt: The serpent with a bold, boastful expression leaning toward Eve who looks surprised and uncertain. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: For God doth know that in the day ye eat thereof, then your eyes shall be opened, and ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil.
verses.little_bible: The serpent said, 'God knows that if you eat it, you will become wise like God and know everything!'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: The serpent says eating the fruit will make you wise like God.
verses.meaning: The serpent's temptation appealed to pride — the desire to be like God apart from God. This is the root of all sin: wanting to be in charge instead of trusting God.
verses.memory_phrase: Wanting to be like God the wrong way.
verses.prayer: God, help me trust that Your way is better than my own ideas.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever wanted something so much that you forgot it might not be good for you?
verses.family_discussion: Pride — wanting to be our own boss instead of trusting God — is at the heart of sin. How do we cultivate humility and dependence on God in our family?
verses.do_it_today: Think of one thing you want to do your own way. Ask God to help you trust Him instead.
verses.illustration_prompt: The serpent pointing dramatically at the glowing tree with a persuasive look; Eve stares at the fruit with longing eyes. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And when the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was pleasant to the eyes, and a tree to be desired to make one wise, she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat, and gave also unto her husband with her; and he did eat.
verses.little_bible: Eve looked at the fruit. It looked tasty and beautiful and she thought it would make her wise. She took some and ate it — and then gave some to Adam, and he ate it too.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Eve eats the fruit and gives some to Adam. He eats it too.
verses.meaning: Sin entered through a look, a desire, a decision, and an action. Disobedience rarely happens all at once — it starts with what we choose to keep looking at.
verses.memory_phrase: They chose to disobey God.
verses.prayer: God, help me look away from things that could lead me to disobey You.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever wanted something so much you forgot it was wrong? What happened?
verses.family_discussion: Sin often follows the path: see → desire → take. How can our family help each other pause at the 'desire' step before making a wrong choice?
verses.do_it_today: When you want something today, ask: 'Would God be happy with this choice?' before you take it.
verses.illustration_prompt: Eve reaching up to take a bright, beautiful fruit from the tree; Adam stands nearby watching. Their expressions show excitement mixed with something uneasy. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked; and they sewed fig leaves together, and made themselves aprons.
verses.little_bible: After they ate, something felt wrong. They felt ashamed and tried to cover themselves with leaves they sewed together.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: They felt ashamed and tried to cover themselves with leaves.
verses.meaning: Sin brings shame — a broken awareness that something is wrong between us and God. Our instinct is to hide or cover up, but that never truly heals the problem.
verses.memory_phrase: Sin made them feel ashamed.
verses.prayer: God, when I do something wrong, help me come to You instead of hiding.
verses.discussion_question: How does it feel inside when you do something you know is wrong?
verses.family_discussion: Shame makes us want to hide. How do we help our children come to God (and to us) with their mistakes rather than hiding them?
verses.do_it_today: If you have done something wrong today, tell God about it right now and ask for His forgiveness.
verses.illustration_prompt: Adam and Eve looking sad and worried, holding large fig leaves, surrounded by lush garden plants. Their expressions show shame and sadness. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And they heard the voice of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the day: and Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God amongst the trees of the garden.
verses.little_bible: Then they heard God walking in the garden in the cool evening breeze. Adam and Eve were scared, so they hid behind the trees.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Adam and Eve hear God coming and hide behind trees.
verses.meaning: Before their sin, walking with God was a joy. Now it brought fear. Sin always damages our friendship with God — but God still came to them, showing He had not given up on them.
verses.memory_phrase: They hid from God.
verses.prayer: God, help me never want to hide from You. Help me come to You when I am afraid.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever hidden from someone because you felt bad? How did it feel?
verses.family_discussion: God came to walk with Adam and Eve even after they sinned. What does this tell us about how God pursues us even when we run from Him?
verses.do_it_today: Instead of hiding when something goes wrong today, say a quick prayer: 'God, I need You.'
verses.illustration_prompt: Adam and Eve crouching behind thick bushes and tall trees, peeking anxiously; warm golden light filters through the garden suggesting God's presence nearby. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And the LORD God called unto Adam, and said unto him, Where art thou?
verses.little_bible: God called out to Adam and said, 'Where are you?'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God calls out, 'Where are you?'
verses.meaning: God was not asking because He didn't know — He is all-knowing. He called because He wanted Adam to come to Him. God always comes looking for us.
verses.memory_phrase: God comes looking for us.
verses.prayer: God, thank You for always looking for me even when I hide.
verses.discussion_question: Why do you think God called 'Where are you?' if He already knew where Adam was?
verses.family_discussion: God's question 'Where are you?' is an invitation, not a trap. How do we communicate that same grace to our children when they've done something wrong?
verses.do_it_today: Say to God today: 'Here I am, God. I don't want to hide from You.'
verses.illustration_prompt: Soft warm light streaming through the trees of the garden; God's gentle, searching presence implied by the light while Adam crouches behind a bush looking uncertain. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And he said, I heard thy voice in the garden, and I was afraid, because I was naked; and I hid myself.
verses.little_bible: Adam said, 'I heard You, God, and I was scared. I felt ashamed, so I hid.'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Adam says he was scared and ashamed, so he hid.
verses.meaning: Adam's response reveals what sin does to us: it replaces love and trust with fear and shame. Sin broke the easy friendship between God and people.
verses.memory_phrase: Sin makes us afraid and ashamed.
verses.prayer: God, help me know that You love me even when I feel ashamed.
verses.discussion_question: What makes you feel scared or ashamed? Can you tell God about it?
verses.family_discussion: Fear and shame are the fruit of broken relationship with God. How does the gospel — knowing God loves us completely in Christ — change how we face our failures?
verses.do_it_today: Tell someone you trust about one thing that made you feel ashamed recently. Let them show you grace.
verses.illustration_prompt: Adam standing partly hidden behind a tree, head bowed, arms wrapped around himself; a gentle golden light shines in his direction. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And he said, Who told thee that thou wast naked? Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou shouldest not eat?
verses.little_bible: God asked Adam, 'Who told you that you should be ashamed? Did you eat from the tree I told you not to eat from?'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God asks Adam, 'Did you eat from the tree I said not to touch?'
verses.meaning: God already knew what Adam had done, but He gave Adam the chance to confess it. Honest confession is always better than making excuses.
verses.memory_phrase: God gives us a chance to be honest.
verses.prayer: God, when I do something wrong, help me tell the truth.
verses.discussion_question: Has a grown-up ever asked you something they already knew the answer to? Why do you think they asked?
verses.family_discussion: Why is it important to confess what we've done wrong rather than wait to be found out? How does confession restore relationship?
verses.do_it_today: If there is something you have been hiding, confess it to God and ask for His forgiveness.
verses.illustration_prompt: Adam looking down, caught and uncomfortable; a warm, firm but gentle divine light surrounds him in the garden. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And the man said, The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat.
verses.little_bible: Adam said, 'The woman You gave me — she gave me the fruit, and I ate it.' He was blaming Eve instead of saying sorry.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Adam blames Eve instead of saying sorry.
verses.meaning: Adam's first reaction was to blame someone else — even blaming God indirectly ('the woman You gave me'). Making excuses instead of taking responsibility makes things worse, not better.
verses.memory_phrase: It is better to say sorry than to blame others.
verses.prayer: God, help me say sorry instead of blaming others when I do wrong.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever blamed someone else when something was your fault? How did that feel?
verses.family_discussion: Adam blamed Eve and even subtly blamed God. How do we cultivate a culture of honest ownership in our family rather than blame-shifting?
verses.do_it_today: If you did something wrong today, say 'I'm sorry' without saying 'but'.
verses.illustration_prompt: Adam pointing toward Eve with a guilty expression; Eve looks upset. Both stand apart in the garden. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And the LORD God said unto the woman, What is this that thou hast done? And the woman said, The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat.
verses.little_bible: God asked Eve, 'What have you done?' Eve said, 'The serpent tricked me, and I ate the fruit.'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Eve says the serpent tricked her.
verses.meaning: Eve blamed the serpent, which was partly true — but she still chose to eat. When we are tricked into doing wrong, we are still responsible for our own choices.
verses.memory_phrase: We are responsible for our own choices.
verses.prayer: God, help me make good choices even when others try to trick me.
verses.discussion_question: Is it okay to blame someone else if they tricked you? Why or why not?
verses.family_discussion: How do we teach children to own their choices, even when someone else influenced them? What does grace look like in that conversation?
verses.do_it_today: Think about a choice you made today. Was it your choice? Tell God about it.
verses.illustration_prompt: Eve gesturing toward the serpent in the tree while speaking to God's soft, present light; her expression is a mix of guilt and explanation. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And the LORD God said unto the serpent, Because thou hast done this, thou art cursed above all cattle, and above every beast of the field; upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt thou eat all the days of thy life:
verses.little_bible: God told the serpent, 'Because you did this, you are cursed. You will crawl on your belly and eat dust for the rest of your life.'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God tells the serpent it is cursed and must crawl on the ground.
verses.meaning: God addressed the serpent first and gave the harshest judgment — the deceiver bears great responsibility. Evil does not win; God holds all things to account.
verses.memory_phrase: God judges those who do evil.
verses.prayer: God, thank You that You are fair and that evil does not win.
verses.discussion_question: What does it mean that God punished the serpent? Was that fair?
verses.family_discussion: God judged each party — serpent, Eve, Adam — individually. How does knowing God is a just judge bring comfort and also challenge us?
verses.do_it_today: Draw a picture of a snake crawling on the ground. Remember: God is in charge, not the enemy.
verses.illustration_prompt: The serpent now on the ground, no longer on a branch, looking diminished and lowly; dirt and dust around it in the garden. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed; it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel.
verses.little_bible: God made a wonderful promise: one day, someone from Eve's family would come and crush the serpent's power — and that person is Jesus!
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God promises that someone from Eve's family will defeat the serpent. That is Jesus!
verses.meaning: This is the first promise of a Saviour in the whole Bible. God promised that Jesus — born of a woman — would defeat Satan. This is the very first gospel message.
verses.memory_phrase: God promised a Saviour — Jesus!
verses.prayer: God, thank You for promising to send Jesus even at the very beginning.
verses.discussion_question: God made a promise to fix everything. Who did He send to keep that promise?
verses.family_discussion: Verse 15 is the proto-evangelium — the first gospel. How amazing that God announced Jesus in the very first book of the Bible! How does this verse change how you read all of Scripture?
verses.do_it_today: Tell someone today: 'God promised to send Jesus even at the very beginning of the Bible!'
verses.illustration_prompt: A beam of warm golden light breaking through the garden's trees; a subtle image of a child's footprint above a serpent's head, symbolising victory. Hope-filled storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: Unto the woman he said, I will greatly multiply thy sorrow and thy conception; in sorrow thou shalt bring forth children; and thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee.
verses.little_bible: God told Eve that because of what happened, life would now be harder in many ways.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God tells Eve life will be harder now.
verses.meaning: Sin brought real consequences into the world — pain and struggle entered into family life and relationships. These are not God abandoning us but showing us how seriously sin damages what He made good.
verses.memory_phrase: Sin makes life harder.
verses.prayer: God, even when life is hard, help me remember You are still with me.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever noticed that life can be hard sometimes? Who can we turn to when things are hard?
verses.family_discussion: The consequences of sin affected relationships and family life. How do we point our children toward God's grace and redemption in the midst of life's real difficulties?
verses.do_it_today: If someone in your family is having a hard day, do one small kind thing to help them.
verses.illustration_prompt: Eve with a gentle, sad expression looking into the distance; the garden around her still beautiful but a few leaves fallen. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And unto Adam he said, Because thou hast hearkened unto the voice of thy wife, and hast eaten of the tree, of which I commanded thee, saying, Thou shalt not eat of it: cursed is the ground for thy sake; in toil shalt thou eat of it all the days of thy life;
verses.little_bible: God told Adam that because he disobeyed, the ground itself would become difficult to work. Getting food would take a lot of hard work from now on.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God tells Adam that work will be much harder now.
verses.meaning: Adam's disobedience affected not just himself but the whole created world. Sin has consequences that ripple outward — but hard work is still dignified and God-given.
verses.memory_phrase: Disobedience brings hard consequences.
verses.prayer: God, help me obey You so that I can live in the good things You made for me.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever noticed that some things are very hard work? Why do you think that is?
verses.family_discussion: Work was originally a blessing (Gen 2); sin made it toilsome. How do we help our children find meaning and dignity in hard work while pointing them to the rest Jesus offers?
verses.do_it_today: Help with one chore at home today and thank God that work can still be good.
verses.illustration_prompt: Adam kneeling near dry, rocky ground trying to plant seeds; his expression shows effort and tiredness. The garden in the background is still beautiful but beyond a boundary. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: Thorns also and thistles shall it bring forth to thee; and thou shalt eat the herb of the field;
verses.little_bible: The ground would now grow thorns and thistles, making farming harder. Adam would still find food, but it would take great effort.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: The ground grows thorns now. Food takes hard work.
verses.meaning: Even creation was affected by sin — the world became resistant and painful to work with. This is part of what Paul calls creation 'groaning' in Romans 8.
verses.memory_phrase: Sin changed the whole world.
verses.prayer: God, thank You that one day You will make everything new again.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever stepped on a thorn or thistle? It hurts! How does that remind us that sin has consequences?
verses.family_discussion: Creation itself was affected by the Fall. How does the hope of a new creation (Rev 21-22) give us perspective on the world's brokenness today?
verses.do_it_today: Go outside and find something thorny or prickly. Think about how much God loves us to still have a plan to fix everything.
verses.illustration_prompt: Close-up of a patch of ground with thorns and thistles growing among ordinary plants; a small sprout of green food plant visible too — hardship and hope together. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread, till thou return unto the ground; for out of it wast thou taken: for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return.
verses.little_bible: God told Adam that he would work very hard all his life, and one day his body would return to the ground — because God made him from dust.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God says Adam came from the ground and will return to it one day.
verses.meaning: God reminded Adam of his creaturely humility — we are made from dust and return to it. This is a serious truth about mortality, but it points us toward our need for a Saviour.
verses.memory_phrase: God made us, and we need Him.
verses.prayer: God, remind me that You are God and I am not. Help me trust You with my whole life.
verses.discussion_question: What does it mean that God made us and knows everything about us?
verses.family_discussion: Our mortality is part of the Fall's consequence, but for believers it leads to resurrection. How do we speak about death to children with both honesty and hope?
verses.do_it_today: Pick up a handful of soil and remember: God made us carefully and loves us completely.
verses.illustration_prompt: Adam looking at his hands while holding a small amount of soil; a look of humility and quiet sorrow on his face, with warm light around him. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And Adam called his wife's name Eve; because she was the mother of all living.
verses.little_bible: Adam named his wife Eve, because she would be the mother of all the people who would ever be born.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Adam names his wife Eve — she is the mother of all people.
verses.meaning: Even in the middle of judgment, Adam named his wife with hope — 'Eve' means 'life.' Adam trusted God's promise that life would continue. Faith looks forward even in hard times.
verses.memory_phrase: Hope lives even in hard times.
verses.prayer: God, thank You for giving us hope even when things are hard.
verses.discussion_question: Why do you think Adam gave his wife a name that means 'life' right after such a hard moment?
verses.family_discussion: Naming Eve in this moment was an act of faith in God's promise. How do we model hope and forward-looking faith to our children when things are hard?
verses.do_it_today: Ask a parent what your name means. Thank God for the person He made you to be.
verses.illustration_prompt: Adam gently placing his hand on Eve's shoulder with a caring, hopeful expression; both standing close together in the garden. Soft, warm light surrounds them. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: Unto Adam also and to his wife did the LORD God make coats of skins, and clothed them.
verses.little_bible: Then God did something beautiful — He made real clothes for Adam and Eve to wear, to cover their shame. Even though they had disobeyed, God still took care of them.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God makes clothes for Adam and Eve and takes care of them.
verses.meaning: God covered their shame with clothes He made Himself. This is a picture of grace — even in judgment, God provides. An animal gave its life so they could be covered, foreshadowing how Jesus would one day die to cover our sin.
verses.memory_phrase: God covers our shame with His love.
verses.prayer: God, thank You for covering my shame and taking care of me — just like You did for Adam and Eve.
verses.discussion_question: Why is it special that GOD made the clothes for Adam and Eve, not them?
verses.family_discussion: God's provision of clothing is one of the Bible's first pictures of grace — He covers what we cannot cover ourselves. How does this point us to what Jesus does for our sin?
verses.do_it_today: When you put on clothes today, say a short prayer: 'Thank You, God, for taking care of me.'
verses.illustration_prompt: God's gentle hands placing warm, cosy coats on Adam and Eve who stand humbly with grateful expressions; soft golden light fills the scene. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And the LORD God said, Behold, the man is become as one of us, to know good and evil: and now, lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever:
verses.little_bible: God said, 'Now the man knows good and evil. We must not let him eat from the tree of life and live forever in this broken way.'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God says people must not eat from the tree of life now.
verses.meaning: God's action was not cruel — living forever in a broken, sinful state would be a terrible thing. He was protecting them from eternal brokenness while also preserving the hope of full restoration.
verses.memory_phrase: God protects us even with hard choices.
verses.prayer: God, help me trust that Your decisions are always good for me, even when they are hard.
verses.discussion_question: Can you think of a time a rule felt hard but was actually there to keep you safe?
verses.family_discussion: God's restriction here was an act of mercy, not meanness. How do we help our children see God's firm boundaries as loving protection rather than rejection?
verses.do_it_today: Thank God for one rule in your family that you know keeps you safe.
verses.illustration_prompt: A glowing tree in the distance with soft light around it; God's presence indicated by warm light on Adam and Eve who look toward the tree from a distance. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: Therefore the LORD God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, to till the ground from whence he was taken.
verses.little_bible: So God sent Adam and Eve out of the Garden of Eden to live in the wider world, where they would work the ground.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God sends Adam and Eve out of the beautiful garden.
verses.meaning: Leaving the garden was the consequence of sin — they were sent away from the most intimate place of fellowship with God. But God did not abandon them; He sent them to live and work in His world.
verses.memory_phrase: Sin separates us, but God does not abandon us.
verses.prayer: God, even when I face hard consequences, help me know You are still with me.
verses.discussion_question: How do you think Adam and Eve felt leaving the beautiful garden?
verses.family_discussion: Being sent out of Eden is a picture of spiritual separation from God that all people experience through sin. How does the gospel restore the closeness with God that was lost here?
verses.do_it_today: Think about a time you had to leave somewhere you loved. Remember that God was still with you.
verses.illustration_prompt: Adam and Eve walking hand in hand away from the lush garden; the garden glows warmly behind them but they walk forward into a wider, simpler landscape. Soft, bittersweet storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: So he drove out the man; and he placed at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way, to keep the way of the tree of life.
verses.little_bible: God placed mighty angels with a flaming sword at the entrance to the garden to guard the tree of life. The garden could not be entered anymore. But God's promise of a rescuer still stood.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Angels with a flaming sword guard the garden. But God's promise is still true.
verses.meaning: The way back to Eden was blocked — but not forever. Revelation 22 shows the tree of life open again to all who are saved through Jesus. The locked gate of Eden will one day swing wide open through Christ.
verses.memory_phrase: Jesus opens the way back to God.
verses.prayer: Thank You, Jesus, for opening the way back to God. I want to be with You forever.
verses.discussion_question: The garden was closed, but God made a promise. Do you remember what the promise was?
verses.family_discussion: The cherubim guarding Eden echo the cherubim over the Ark of the Covenant — and ultimately the veil torn in the Temple at Jesus' death. How does the whole Bible story move from locked garden to open city (Rev 21-22)?
verses.do_it_today: Draw a picture of the garden with angels at the gate, and then draw Jesus opening the door. Remember: Jesus made a way back to God.
verses.illustration_prompt: Two majestic angels with wings spread wide at the entrance of a glowing garden; between them a turning, flaming sword. Adam and Eve visible in the distance walking away, but a warm horizon ahead of them. Hopeful storybook watercolor style.
